item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm gonna have to agree with Hippo on this one. -1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was worried going into this that it was going to be one of those “now here’s what really happened” articles where we find out AWCY did 9/11 or something. But instead it was tasteful and mostly respectful.

Maybe someone can illuminate for me, but I don’t understand 4911-B’s purpose in the story. I understand what it’s doing but I do t understand why it needed to be an entity separate from 4911-A.

item.title: Moderator Post - CLOSED
item.content:encoded: What a horrible thing to say, and blatant trolling at that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Staff Notice: this user has been permanently banned for trolling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’ll admit, nice inversion of the usual "using the writer's take on the cause of a real-world event that we don’t know the whole truth behind the cause of as a plot point in a science fiction setting" trope. Here, instead of (at least 1 of) the thing(s) that contributed to the occurrence of the real event being the skip (or the event itself being 1), we have a real event causing the skip's existence. This & 3057 are — to my knowledge — the only "real cause to a fictional event" skips to NOT involve a "rational explanation for mythical phenomena" as their SCP gimmick (953, 2863, 2953, 3000, & 3470 being good examples of that).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cue this previously dead forum waking up again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this post.

People die lots. It's pretty sad. 9/11 was pretty sad.

Treating it like a kind of sacred cow just because, for the first time since the Nazis, ground level Americans got a first-hand taste of the pain of the people in the countries we fuck up - and then subsequently, frantically hammered on the SAVE US MEN WITH GUNS button to shift that pain to yet another smorgasbord of third-world farmhands a couple planets away, sat right with me as a child. It doesn't anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was four when it happened, so I don't remember it all that well. I can tell that this article is in good taste and that you have a stronger connection to it. Because of your connection to the event I think that this article is 100% better because unlike most events, some of yourself in this entry, and I respect that. +1 from me all the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wasn't alive at the time the September 11th attacks happened, but I have been around long enough to know the tragedy it was, and how it affected so many people. This article is handled with care, and is done in a way I can't imagine would offend or hurt anyone. That being said, the depth put into the SCP itself is interesting, the description was done to the Foundations best in-universe ability, and this all around has earned every positive vote it has accumulated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Never forget.
9/11 was such a tragic, devastating event, and I was worried that this article was going to either joke about it or exploit what happened at 9/11 to use for some okay-ish SCP. My worries were quickly extinguished. This SCP has such a good execution, and it's not exploiting 9/11 in anyway, which makes it to be a great SCP. And while yes, while 4911-A is a bit Superman-ish, it still makes a good representation of all the people who were helping in the events of 9/11, people who even in tough times, powered through to help and save others. While 4911-B represents the tragedy and despair of 9/11, reminding us that 9/11 was a horrible and sad event. That's how I interpret it anyways.

+1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one doesn't work for me. I think it succeeds at being a piece of fiction about 9/11, approaching the subject with respect and evoking the right emotions in the reader. But I don't think it's a good SCP article, despite of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At face value, I can see where someone walking in and giving this a glance can assume the author's motivations for writing this. Of course we have seen many people riding the coattails of real life events when arguably it could do just fine without going down that road. I can imagine that the entities would have existed just fine if the setting had been an apartment fire or a landslide in a densely populated area or a war zone. Arguably this would have been a more proper setting for the entities to exist in, and if that is truly the case I'm sure it would have been written as such if you just wanted to write about angels/ghosts/personifications of human compassion/etc. doing good in the world during bad times.

No, in this case the setting is a deliberate choice. So then it of course begs the question; why is it a deliberate choice? Obviously the event in question invokes a wide range of emotions in people, American or not. Those of us who vividly remember this time in our lives, weighs on us in some manner. So it is really an impossible feat to be able to write on the subject and expect to appease the majority when so many people have such a general emotional attachment to 9/11. It will invoke a lot of different feelings no matter what. So anyone can conclude that there is no real payout to "cash in on upvotes" when writing about 9/11, and any serious author who says otherwise truly has no connection to their audience. It's a really silly accusation.

I see that the author wrote about their own feelings regarding the event; what they felt like in those days and expressed it. That, more than the entities themselves whom are really just a vehicle for the story, is what this SCP is really about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really dude?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Was expecting a dumb 9/11 joke. Luckily those tend to crash and burn.

Great execution +1

Nice job author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 9/11 occupies a weird head-space for a lot of people (not just Americans!). Having strong feelings about any piece of fiction that deals directly with it (regardless of how well written) isn't unusual. While some of the criticisms here might be a bit inflammatory, plenty more strike me as reasonable and sympathetic. For example, I found spikesbrennan's criticism very compelling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wish this skip really exist…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Give me a break.

It's a well-written, wholesome SCP. I'm sad to see so many people impose traits on this that just aren't there in order to rant on whatever political perspective they have on what is essentially a backdrop to this story, ignoring the point of it entirely. Much of what I've seen here just boils down to "this guy can't write about human good-will because it's controversial", and it's a very tasteless and almost insulting critique.

A hearty +1 from me. Well done, djkaktus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, at first I thought this was a troll scp

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know, I doubt the SCP-wiki would have succeeded as well as it did without 9/11. The Foundation echoes the Department of Homeland Security — a organization with tremendous power, little oversight, and the goal of doing "whatever needs to be done" to fight a perceived existential threat. From this, you get "extralegal detention" (D-Class), drone warfare, NSA surveillance, and absolute authority (Overseers) — all classic Foundation tropes. There's even the whole classified information angle — sure, things like "The reason it's classified is also classified" predates 9/11, but it was in the years following 9/11 that this notion gained so much traction in the American consciousness. The CIA MKUltra stuff that got released that very same year probably helped.

Anyway!

Long Rant Ensues
Seriously this ended up being way longer than I intended

My issue with this is that the event has been exploited by so many people in so many ways for so many reasons that it's almost impossible to address narratively without playing into this exploitation. It's an event enshrined in American culture — where it has been endlessly romanticized, monetized, and weaponized into political capital. Political capital that has purchased some extremely awful, terrible, monstrous things.

I think this article makes all the right moves that it can: It focuses on first responders, doesn't make 9/11 magic, and avoids doing that godawful Marvel thing (just imagine SCP-682 lifting its head in its cell and shedding a single tear for the WTC. I think I would fucking die). That being said, it still is precisely what it is: An article where abstract forces manifest at ground zero to save American lives. Because this time? It's personal.

Wholly unintentionally, it reinforces the narrative that the excesses taken in the wake of 9/11 were justified — from an unsanctioned and purposeless war in Iraq to the torture and murder at Guantanamo Bay, all the way up to the modern-day anti-immigration policies of the Trump administration. Because this tragedy was special — exceptional, even. And exceptional tragedies require exceptional measures.

But 9/11 wasn't actually special. Stuff like this happens all the time, all around the world. The reason it's so deeply enshrined in our culture has less to do with its scale and more to do with the fact that we Americans are accustom to feeling invincible. The way we perceive 9/11 plays into our beliefs in regards to American exceptionalism — that we are unique, special, invulnerable; that to do us harm is to spit in the eye of God.

All that being said, I think I understand the spirit behind the article. The idea here is that these abstract forces appeared in response to the selflessness of the first responders. Having worked alongside emergency response teams, I can appreciate that; emergency responders are pretty awesome, and a group of people who should be celebrated way more than they are (seriously, how many occupations out there require you to run toward the sound of explosions and gunfire?). On that merit, I liked the article, and I wouldn't downvote.

But because of the context surrounding the article — which is no fault of the author himself, but nevertheless informs how I read this — I can't upvote. I can't divorce this thing from Giuliani mythologizing the tragedy of 9/11 for political gain, or the jingoistic hawks who turned this tragic-but-comprehensible event into a series of absurd and pointless wars, or how our continued refusal to understand how something like 9/11 was inevitable (not justified, and not deserved — but nevertheless, still inevitable) given our dangerous imperialistic policies has provided pro-authoritarian ideologues with the perfect opportunity to shill their fascist pigshit.

Wow, this ended up a lot longer than I expected. Anyway, uh — tl;dr: I think this is a fine article and I think the author clearly put tremendous care in making it as respectful as possible, but I also think that because of the context of 9/11 there's really no way to execute this idea that I'd be comfortable with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It feels like you're saying "oh these people were heroes, but only with the SCP being there to encourage them to do so."

The intent was actually the exact opposite of this. These two only manifested because there were heroes, not the other way round.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel as if I should give my 2 cents on this article.

While I originally upvoted the article, I changed to a novote, because while it is an incredibly well written article, I'm not so sure if something such as 9/11 should be written about in such a way. Now I will say that I do laugh at 9/11 memes and have done things that would be insensitive, but the article is about these people being saved by an SCP, which feels… odd. It feels like you're saying "oh these people were heroes, but only with the SCP being there to encourage them to do so."

Sorry if my wording is weird or doesn't make sense, I can't quite describe why the article makes me feel so. It's a very weird feeling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My father would often go to the WTC for repairs in the late 90s

My mother worked not too far from there on the day everything went down

She told me that she called my dad a few minutes after the planes hit and had a heart attack when he didn't pick up. Thankfully, he did pick up the second time she called and she informed him of everything

When I think over it, I was this close to having one of my parents die and me never being born.

That being said, I have to reluctantly downvote this. Do I think anyedd of you kaktus because you wrote this? No. I am downvoting because I believe this doesn't do justice for an event that shaped my childhood despite me not being alive during that time.

item.title: Re: MOD POST - INFO - OPEN
item.content:encoded: Okay, I understand the first paragraph was disrespectful. But I don't like the fact of using an specific slot and publishing in a certain date to increase the upvotes of THIS skip. It is not a 231 / 2317.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I keep seeing this criticism come up, and while I feel the 4911 slot and publishing on 9/11 is pretty tasteless, I disagree with placing this at any other disaster. The author's premise was based on the manifestation of the citizens' unity, valor etc (based on my own interpretation as well as the author's comments) as distinctly American icons. I think that's the key. And the crass commercialization and co-opting of the tragedy for political purposes disgusts me as much as anyone, but I honestly feel that this was coming from a pretty pure place. And believe me, I don't have an ounce of patriotic feeling for the United States.

This could have been done in a different place with a different scenario, and that would be fantastic, but it would be different icons and thus a different article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like there wasn't anything in this that needed to be about 9/11, you could've taken the same elements and placed them in any other disaster. To me, it's the equivalent of writing an SCP about a school shooting and posting it on the anniversary of Columbine. Shock value and trying to get attention for the article without really having it need to be about the subject material.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Clever, controversial, (un)contained.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be fair, most of those are where the event itself (or something that somehow contributed to it happening) was (at least 1 of) the skip(s). Here, the event's occurrence brought the skip into being.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i don't get it. it was just a ghost, to me, I think. but the dialogue was highly realistic and you cheated by using fancy graphics so I voted up

item.title: MOD POST - INFO - OPEN
item.content:encoded:
Tepir, be mindful that your critique crosses over to the point of personal attack.
Saying things like "I think the author wanted to write about 9/11 but had no idea what to do and came up with this" is essentially passing judgment on the author directly by denigrating their personal effort in this article, while I think this only stays because it is about 9/11 plus the 4911 slot casts doubt not just on the quality of the article, but on the integrity of the user base and the voting system.
Per our Criticism Policy, please refrain from making such statements and focus on the content of the article itself.

item.title: Re: MOD POST - INFO - CLOSED
item.content:encoded:
What's "allowing them" is that I wasn't called to the thread to deal with them being an ass, I was called to this thread to deal with a long-time author blatantly calling another user "stupid". I'm extremely ill today. I posted here as a favor to another staff member.

I'm gonna make this clear. No more low-level comment sniping. No more passive-aggressive line toeing. Here, or anywhere else.

To make this double clear, I expect better from you, because you know better.

If you feel like someone needs to be staffposted on, you should contact staff, not make vague inferences about staff intentions.

This is a closed staff post. Do not respond unless you are staff.

item.title: Re: MOD POST - INFO - OPEN
item.content:encoded: So what's allowing them to take shots at me in the first place, if the inverse warrants a staff post? Should people who say stupid things in comment threads that will be there forever not be held accountable for those things? Because staff doesn't seem interested in doing so.

item.title: MOD POST - INFO - OPEN
item.content:encoded:
Having someone take shots at you doesn't allow you to take shots back at them.

There's been a lot of low-level passive(and active) aggression in your responses to this thread overall.

Take a breath, Kaktus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can't fix stupid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's more on the people doing the coopting.

Given that it's 17 years later and nobody can reasonably say 9/11 victims haven't had ample positive media representation, I don't think my lack of appreciation for another story about them is going to do them any significant harm, in contrast to the perpetual milking of the tragedy for political purposes. So, no, I'm not going to try much harder.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This comes off as pretty insensitive, especially given the original Author Post kaktus made at the beginning of this thread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And ofc it's Kaktus.

Better than the alternative. I could be spending my time calling authors lazy in discussion threads.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the author wanted to write about 9/11 but had no idea what to do and came up with this

Well that's just incorrect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: -1.I think the author wanted to write about 9/11 but had no idea what to do and came up with this, second image looks so fake it made me cringe. I think this only stays because it is about 9/11 plus the 4911 slot. I really really hope this gets rewritten.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
lol better write an SCP article about major political events :)

And ofc it's Kaktus.

Edit since I know someone is going to get upset:
You can't write about major political events in the fashion and get mad that people are accusing you of piggybacking off of said political event. Personally, I think it's lazy to use something as widely known as this, that gets shoved into our faces at least once a year every year.

There might not be a written rule, but I thought this site was dedicated to science-fiction, not trying to make an anomaly out of something that was actually present (human spirit). I'm glad other people are also giving some push-back too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm trying to figure out why, exactly, I dislike this. I usually like sad, emotional pieces.

I guess because the core concept isn't overly interesting. I'm not sure how well this would stand up by itself without the emotional investment that many people have in the 9/11 attacks, and because of that I feel as if this is vaguely exploitative. I know that wasn't your intent, but without some more actual content for me to sink my teeth into, I don't feel I can upvote this.

Also, it feels quite long for how little story is actually there. I have always appreciated short, sweet entries, so I am tempted to downvote. However this is nowhere near as bad as some of the other offenders.

Novote from me.

Edit: I'm actually going to downvote. It simply wasn't an enjoyable read. The only real positive thing I can say about it is that you have very good prose, and while that is important, it cant carry an article when there is nothing else going for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The image header.png is taken from here, and is used under Fair Use as a creative commentary on the original image.

I don't think that's allowable under the copyright license we use, is it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is very silly, unfortunate, and hurtful that you are unable to separate the innocent victims of 9/11 from the political agenda they were coopted for. Understandable, maybe, but you could try a little harder.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually generally agree with you, but ran into the comment of "I don't know what the fuck is going on and that's bad" in crit. What you're seeing is probably an overreaction to that.

item.title: Re: How do we handle spoilers, again?
item.content:encoded:
How do we handle spoilers, again?

The simplest way is usually with a

Collapsible
The US Pledge of Allegiance

I pledge allegiance to my Flag
and the Republic for which it stands,
one nation, indivisible,
with liberty and justice for all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kind of hard to critique an article like this, what with all the baggage from many different directions, as evidenced by this being maybe the fourth attempt I've made at writing this comment.

I feel like this oversells a concept that works far better if it is undersold, and that this would benefit greatly from being a lot sparser and shorter. I actually really like the central conceit - there were anomalies seen during the 9/11 attacks but the Foundation don't have enough information to say anything about them conclusively. It highlights the incomplete mesh of overlapping personal narratives, the monumental news coverage, and the dual sense of both the immediacy and irretrievable distance that, for me, characterize the way 9/11 has been covered and remembered. That material really works here because the Foundation is obsessive in its attempt to recover information - faced with an incident whose scope and catastrophe frustrate all such recoveries, they are left spinning their wheels, their helplessness mirroring everyone else's in the face of such an event.

Unfortunately, the emotional thrust of the story works at cross purposes with the material that I appreciate the most here, and leads to the entities (particularly -A) being overexposed and losing the sense of irretrievability and inexplicability which -B, for the most part, retains. I also wish I hadn't read the comments implying that these are Lady Liberty and Lady Justice, which I don't like anywhere near as much as not knowing what these things are supposed to be and diminishes the article for me. The actual story here (spirits summoned by the collective goodness of the people of New York tried to intercede to save people) comes through a little too overtly, and could have benefited from a lighter touch.

item.title: How do we handle spoilers, again?
item.content:encoded:
Well, since I don't remember, I'll just give you a very strong hint:

What is the last line of the Pledge of Allegiance?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The article was done as tastefully as it could have been done, considering the subject matter. Its slot and the reservation thereof strike me as immensely tacky, but have-at-it.

As for the content itself, it just didn't land for me. Something about the logs strike me as off. They're the meat of the article, but they don't feel substantial. Like the characters are there to exposit, to vaguely point at the themes of the article. The themes that are carried less so by the execution, and moreso by the concept itself. There's nothing in the text itself that makes me go, "Yes, the spirit of the people of New York City is indomitable."

This part is incredibly subjective, but I don't feel like this article has any real pathos of its own. To me, it kinda rides off of the original event, using a pre-existing investment in the reader to create feeling, rather than invoking any directly. The closest it gets is -B is being unable to actually save the jumpers, which is decently done, but that can't carry the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd say the refusal or inability of some Americans to consider that the lionisation of 9/11 responders might come across as pro-American propaganda, even if not intended as such, is just as much an example of projected bias.

What if you wrote about heroic self-sacrifice, but made it about an American soldier in Iraq jumping on a grenade or running into a collapsing building? It could be a completely uncontroversial act of heroism, with all political references excised from the setting, and make no reference to the actual war, but even so claims that 'it's just a tribute to heroes/about the human spirit/it's not pro American at all' should be treated with a great deal of scepticism.

9/11 has similar connotations, as far as I'm concerned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would still insist that you're reading too far and too deep to this article—reaching conclusion beyond its scope. But you do you, it's obvious my thoughts are not reaching you.

If you don't like that only murricans have angels, by all means write the saviors for ISIS victims, Bali bombings, or Aceh tsunami. But this isn't about them. Only because there are other tragedies doesn't mean this one tragedy must be of lesser importance. Heck, if we're talking about scope, the hurricanes would be larger than the attack, yet this article only explicitly mentioned this one incident. Perhaps there's something to consider in that fact.

That's all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't help but feel like most of your complaints is projected bias.

I've read through the article multiple times, and while, yes it is centered around the events of 9/11, the other things you mention just…aren't there.

There's no "Ra ra americanness" going on, in my eyes.

Reading the post which I'm responding to, there's a lot of personal interpretation you're putting out, almost all of which is pretty glaringly slanted.

I get that you don't like this, but it's really hard to take your opinion seriously when you spent seven paragraphs railing against a bunch of supposition you put on the article, and basically diatribe against Americans/The 'states et al.

Almost everything you said was a reaction to the subject matter/location, instead of addressing any of the actual writing weaknesses of the article. (Which there are a few.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah it's about New York, which is in America. Kind of my point. I'm not advocating for the removal of it, i'm just stating why my downvote and how the intention of the writer is not always (or never, even) the same as the underlying message. My comment is precisely about how this is only "great" because it's about americans being awesome, and not about the human spirit, regardless of what the author says. Make this about any other catastrophe, release it on a random day, maybe even have a not so famous author publish it… I sincerely dont believe it would get +10 votes. It has great prose, like all of kaktus' stuff, but that's it.

Bush vs Gore is a story with american politics, but not about american politics, for example. This article misses the point entirely. All my opinion obviously, but ever since I studied semiotics I can't see things like this and not see what's underneath. You could argue my analysis is wrong, but not that it's the product of a narrow vision. Saying "oh it's just about people being nice and the human spirit" is the narrow vision i'm talking about.

This is figuratively about americans summoning two angels to help them when they needed them, by sheer power of will and being fucking 'muricans. I guess the survivors of the Tokyo bombings didnt have the same willpower, or just werent worthy. this is precisely about New York. It's cathartic for newyorkers, for americans, and for nothing else. It's just clickbait. It's not a good article.

Edit:

It's about a group of people, confused, wounded, yet still have in their heart to help others. Were there similar heroes in wars and disasters? Sure. But it's not about them. This is a story about particular heroes from particular tragedy.

I'm not arguing against this. I agree entirely. I dont believe it deserves an upvote in its current state, though. It's like making an article about a real person. Usually downvoted to hell. But this one isnt, because of real life feellings, and not because of the actual article.

item.title: Admin post- closed
item.content:encoded:
If you have an issue with a user, please report this to staff. Otherwise this is backseat modding. Please consider this post also as a response to Rigen and Rednose.

DrFunky, please show more respect for the subject matter at hand. There is a time and place for memes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’m gonna be honest, I like it but I feel like A was mentioned too much and B wasn’t enough. I’m gonna have to novote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it’s a very tasteless one.

Sort of shitty to make a comment like this without elaborating as to why you think that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is basically it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was very nervous about what the narrative would be for this article. I am relieved to say I do not find this disrespectful, as it is not explaining away 9/11 with anomalous conspiracies (what I was a little worried about) but rather introducing these figures who appear to be representative of the bravery of the real responders themselves. None of the real tragedy is downplayed in this article — it is all still referenced — and I interpreted it as a salute to the first responders and the bravery of those affected.

(That's why I cried, at least.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You joined 20 days ago, and your first words is this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're not being clear on this. What exactly is so tasteless about it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, in my eyes this gets a pass because the skip isn't 9/11 itself, it's just an anomaly that occurred during it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’m sick of the major world events really being SCP things trope, if this is to be a touchstone of that particular genre it’s a very tasteless one.
